title: 第 46 節
en: neither black nor white, it produces no fruit, because it is performed without any desire.

As the results of actions we have Vasanas, impressions, or Samskaras, dispositions. They show themselves either in what remains, often dormant, and is then called memory1, or in the peculiar genus, of man, bird, cow, Brahman or Sudra, in the locality and in the time when a man is born. These remainders never cease, so that the animal propensities may lie dormant for a time in a Brahman, but break out again when he enters on a canine birth. They are not said to be without beginning, because desires and fears can only arise when there are objects to be feared or desired (IV, 10).- Impressions are caused by perceptions, perceptions spring from desire, desire from nescience. The Result of them all is the body with its instincts, their habitat the mind, their support, or that on which they lean, the same as the support of perception, i. e. the objective world. Hence it is said that they sprout, like seeds, but that by Knowledge and Yoga they can be annihilated also like seeds, when roasted. In connexion with this the question is discussed, how anything can ever be completely destroyed, how what exists can be made not to exist, and how what does not exist can be made to exist. I doubt, however, whether Eajendralal Mitra can be right (III, 9, IV, 13) when he discovers here something like the theory of ideas <or logoi in the mind of Pata/?^ali, and holds that the three ways or Adhvans in which objects present themselves to the mind, or affect the mind, as past, present and future, correspond to the admission of universalia ante rem, the ideas or types, the universalia in re, the essence, and the universalia post rem, the concepts in our minds. I confess I hardly understand his meaning. It should never be forgotten that the mind is taken by Pata/?</ali as by itself unconscious (not as Svabhasa, self-illuminated, IV, 18) and as becoming conscious and intelligent for a time only by the union between it and the Purusha, who is pure intelligence. The Manas only receives the consciousness of perception which comes in reality from the Purusha, so that here we 1 This kind of memory comes very near to what we call instinct, propensity, or untaught ability.

IS YOGA NIHILISM? 359 should have the etymological, though somewhat fanciful, definition of consciousness (con-scientia) as well as of the Sanskrit Sam-vid, i. e. knowing along with the mind, i. e. apprehending the impressions of the mind (SvabuddhiSamvedanam). But though .fiatta is the work of the Manas, not directly of the Btiddhi, this .STitta, when seen by the seer (Purusha) on one side and tinged with what is seen on the other, may be spoken of as the thought of the Purusha, though it is so by a temporary misconception only. This _5Titta again is coloured by many former impressions (Vasana). It may be called the highest form of Prakriti, and as such it serves no purpose of its own, but works really for another, the Purusha, whom it binds and fascinates for a time with the sole purpose, we are told, of bringing him back to a final recognition of his true Self (IV, 24).
Kaivalya.

If that is once achieved, the Purusha knows that he himself is not experiencer, neither knower nor actor ; and the Manas or active mind, when beginning to feel the approach of Kaivalya, turns more and more inward and away from the world, so as not to interfere with the obtainment of the highest bliss of the Purusha. Yet there is always danger of a relapse in unguarded moments or in the intervals of meditation. Old impressions may reassert themselves, and the mind may lose its steadiness, unless the old Yoga-remedies are used again and again to remove all impediments. Then at last, perfect discrimination is rewarded by what is called by a strange term, Dharmamegha-, the cloud of virtue, knowledge and virtue being inseparable like cause and effect. All works and all sufferings have now ceased, even what is to be known becomes smaller and smaller, the very GuTias, i.e. Prakriti, having done their work, cease troubling; Purusha becomes himself, is independent, undisturbed, free, and blessed.
Is Yog-a Mlfcilism ?
This is the end of the Yoga-philosophy, and no wonder that it should have been mistaken for complete nihilism by 36O INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

Cousin and others. But first of all, the play of Prakriti, though it has ceased for our Purusha, who has gained true knowledge, is supposed to be going on for ever for the benefit of other innumerable Purushas ; and as long as there are any spectators, the spectacle of Prakriti will never cease. Secondly, the Purusha, though freed from illusion, is not thereby annihilated. He is himself, apart from nature, and it is possible, though it is not distinctly stated, that the Purusha in his aloneness may continue his life, like the (rivanmukta of the Vedanta, maintaining his freedom among a crowd of slaves, without any fear or hope of another life, unchanged himself in this ever-changing Samsara. However, we need not attempt to supply what Patatfc/ali himself has passed over in silence. The final goal whether of the Yoga, or of the Samkhya, nay even of the Vedanta and of Buddhism, always defies description. Nirva?ia in its highest sense is a name and a thought, but nothing can be predicated of it. It is c what no eye has seen and what has not entered into the mind of man/ We know that it is ; but no one can say what it is, and those who attempt to do so are apt to reduce it to a mere phantasmagoria or to a nothing.

Though I hope that the foregoing sketch may give a correct idea of the general tendency of the Yogaphilosophy, I know but too well that there are several points which require further elucidation, and on which even native expositors hold different opinions. What we must guard against in all these studies is rejecting as absurd whatever we cannot understand at once, or what to us seems fanciful or irrational. I know from my own experience how often what seemed to me for a long time unmeaning, nay absurd, disclosed after a time a far deeper meaning than I should ever have expected.

The great multitude of technical terms, though it may be bewildering to us, could not be entirely suppressed, because it helps to show through how long and continuous a development these Indian systems of thought must have passed, before any attempt was made, as it was by Patawgali and others, to reduce them to systematic order. There remains with me a strong conviction that Indian philoso- IS YOGA NIHILISM? 361 pliers are honest in their reasonings, and never use empty words. But there remains much to be done, and I can only hope that if others follow in my footsteps, they will in time make these old bones to live again. These ancient sages should become fellow-workers and fellow-explorers with ourselves in unknown continents of thought, and we ought not to be afraid to follow in their track. They always have the courage of their convictions, they shrink from no consequences if they follow inevitably from their own premisses. This is the reason why I doubt whether the admission of an Isvara or lord by Pata^^ali, in contradistinction to Kapila who denies that there are any arguments in support of such a being, should be put down as a mere economy or as an accommodation to popular opinion. Indian philosophers are truthful, and Pata%ali (II, 36) says in so many words that truth is better than sacrifice l. They may err, as Plato has erred and even Kant, but they are not decepti deceptores, they do not deceive or persuade themselves, nor do they try to deceive others.
1 SatyapratishifMyam kriyaphalasrayatvat.

NYAYA AND YAISESHIKA. Relation "between Ny&ya and VaisesMia, WHILE In the systems hitherto examined, particularly in the Ved&nta, Samkhya, and Yoga, there runs a strong religious and even poetical vein, we now come to two systems, Nyaya and Vaiseshika, which are very dry and unimaginative, and much more like what we mean by scholastic systems of philosophy, businesslike expositions of what can be known, either of the world which surrounds us or of the world within, that is, of our faculties or powers of perceiving, conceiving, or reasoning on one side, and the objects which they present to us, on the other.

It should be remembered that, like the Samkhya and Yoga, and to a certain extent like the Purva and UttaraMirnamsa, the Ny&ya and Vaiseshika also have by the Hindus themselves been treated as forming but one disci- • pline. We possess indeed a separate body of Nyaya-Sfttras and another of Vaiseshika-Sutras, and these with their reputed authors, Gotama and Kauada, have long been accepted as the original sources whence these two streams of the ancient philosophy of India proceeded. But we know now that the literary style which sprang up naturally in what I called the Siitra-period, the period to which the first attempts at a written, in place of a purely mnemonic, literature may have to be ascribed, was by no means restricted to that ancient period, but continued to be so well imitated in later times that we find it used with great success not only in the Samkhya-Sutras, which are later than Madhava (1350 A.D.), but in more modern compositions also. It should always be borne in mind that the Sfttras ascribed to Gotama and Ka?i4da presuppose a long previous RELATION BETWEEN NY^TA AND VALSESHIKA. 363 development of philosophical thought, and instead of regarding the two as two independent streams, it seems far more likely that there existed at first an as yet undifferentiated body of half philosophical half popular thought, bearing on things that can be known, the Padarthas, i.e. omne scibile, and on the means of acquiring such knowledge, from which at a later time, according to the preponderance of either the one or the other subject, the two systems of Vaiseshika and Nyaya branched off. These two systems shared of course many things in common, and hence we can well understand that at a later time they should have been drawn together again and treated as one, as we see in Sivaditya's Saptapadarthi (about 1400 A.D.), in the Bhasha-Pari/c&Aeda3 with its commentary the Muktavali, in the Tarkasamgraha, the Tarkakamnudi, the Tarkamrita, &c. For practical purposes it is certainly preferable that we should follow their example and thus avoid the necessity of discussing the same subjects twice over. There may have been an old Tarka, very like our Tarkasamgraha, the one before the bifurcation of the old system of Anvikshiki, the other after the confluence of the two. But these are as yet conjectures only, and may have to remain mere conjectures always, so that, in the present state of our knowledge, and depending, as we have to do, chiefly on the existing Sutras as the authorities recognised in India itself, we must not attempt a historical treatment, but treat each system by itself in spite of unavoidable repetitions.

A very zealous native scholar, Mahadeo R&j&ram Bodas, in the Introduction to his edition of the Tarkasamgraha, has indeed promised to give us some kind of history of the Ny&ya-philosophy in India. But unfortunately that period ' in the historical development of the Ny&ya which is of greatest interest to ourselves, namely that which preceded the composition of the Nyaya-Sutras, had by him also to be left a blank, for the simple reason that nothing is known "of Ny&ya before Gotama. The later periods, however, have been extremely well treated by Mr. Sodas, and I may refer my readers to him for the best information on the subject. Mr. Bodas places the Sutras of Gotama and in the fifth or fourth cent. B. c. ; and he expresses 364 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
a belief that the Vaiseshika, nay even the S&mkhya, as systems of thought, were anterior to Buddha, without however adducing any new or certain proofs.

Dates are the weak points in the literary history of India, and, in the present state of our studies, any date, however late, should be welcome. In former years to assign the Kapila-Sutras to the fourteenth or even fifteenth century A.D., would have seemed downright heresy. Was not K&lidasa himself assigned to a period long before the beginning of our era? It seems now generally accepted that K&lidasa really belonged to the sixth century A.D., and this date of Kalid&sa may help us to a date for the Sutras of Gotama, valuable to us, though it may be despised by those who imagine that the value of Sanskrit literature depends chiefly on its supposed remote antiquity. I have pointed out l that, according to native interpreters, Kalid&sa alluded to the logician Dign&ga in a verse of his Meghaduta2. We may suppose therefore that Dignaga was considered a contemporary of Kalid&sa. Now Dign&ga is said by V^/caspati Misra, in his Nyaya-varttika-tatparyatfik&, to have interpreted the Nyaya aphorisms of Gotama in a heterodox or Buddhist sense, while Uddyotakara wrote his commentary to refute his interpretation and to restore that of Pakshilasvamin. If V&fcaspafci Misra is right, we should be allowed to place Dign&ga in the sixth century, and assign the same or rather an earlier date to the Sutras of Gotama, as explained by him and other Ny&ya philosophers. So late a date may not seem to be worth much, still I think it is worth having. Several other dates may be fixed by means of that of Dignaga as I tried to show in the passage quoted above (India, pp. 307 seq.).

A more comprehensive study of Buddhist literature may possibly shed some more light on the chronology of the later literature of the Br&hmans, if I am right in supposing that in the beginning the followers of Buddha broke by no 1 India, p. 307.
2 See also Prof. Satis Chandra VidyabMshana in Journal of Buddhist Text Society, IV, parts iii? and Iv, p. r6.

DIONAGA. 365 means so entirely, as has generally been supposed, witli the literary traditions of the Brahmans. It is quite intelligible why among the various systems of Hindu philosophy the Buddhists should have paid little attention to the two Mim&msas, concerned as they both were with the Veda, an authority which the Buddhists had rejected. But there was no reason why the Buddhists should forswear the study of either the Ny&ya or Vaiseshika systems, or even the Samkhya system, though making their reserves on certain points, such as the existence of an tsvara, which was admitted by the Ny&yas, but denied by Buddha. We know that at the court of Harsha, Brahmans, Bauddhas, and (?ainas were equally welcome (India, pp. 307 seq.). We know from Chinese travellers such as Hiouen-thsang that Vasubandha, for instance, before he became a Buddhist, had read with his master, Vinayabhadra or Samghabhadra *, not only the books of the eighteen schools which were Buddhist, but also the six Tirthya philosophies, clearly meant for the six Br&hmanic systems of philosophy. This Yasubandha, as a very old man, was actually the teacher of Hiouen-thsang, who travelled in India from 629. to 648 A.D. Therefore in Vasubandha's time all the six systems of Indian philosophy must have been in existence, in the form of Sutras or Karik&s. For we possess, in one case at least, a commentary by Pakshila-sv&min or V&tsyayana on the Nyaya-Sutras, the same as those which we possess, and we know that the same Sutras were explained afterwards -by Dignaga, the Buddhist. This Buddhist commentary was attacked by Uddyotakara, a Brahman, of the sixth century, while in the beginning of the seventh century Dharmakirtti, a Buddhist, is said to have defended Dignaga2 and to have criticised Uddyotakara's Nyayav&rttika. In the ninth century Dharmottara, a Buddhist, defended Dharmakirtti's and indirectly Dignaga's interpretation of the Nyaya-Sutras, and it was not till the tenth 1 See also Journal of Buddhist Text Society, 1896, p. 16.

2 Though none of Dignaga's writings have as yet been discovered, Sri Sarat Chandra states that there is in the library of the Grand Lama a Tibetan translation of his Nyaya-samuA&aya (Journal of Buddhist Text Society, part iii7 1896, p. 17).

century that Va&aspati Misra finally re-established the Brahmanic view of the Ny&ya in his Ny&ya-varttikat&tparya-ffldL This would coincide with the period of the Brahmanic reaction and the general collapse of Buddhism in India, and thus place before us an intelligible progress in the study of the Ny&ya both by Brahmans and Buddhists from the sixth to the tenth century, while the revival of the Nyaya dates from Gamgesa Up&dhy&ya who lived in the fourteenth century at Mithila.

Thanks to the labours of Sarat Chandra D&s and Satis Chandra Vidy&bhusha^a, we have lately gained access to some of the Sutras of the Buddhist schools of philosophy, which are full of interest. Of the four great schools of the Buddhists, the M&dhyamika, YogaMra, Sautr&ntika, and VaibMshika, the first or Madhyamika now lies before us in the Madhyamika Vritti by Jfandra-Kirtti, and there is every hope that other philosophical treatises also, for instance, the Ny&ya-samui&aya, may be made accessible to us by the labours of these indefatigable scholars.

The Sutras or rather K&rik&s of the Madhyamika school must, of course, be distinguished from the system of thought which they are meant to explain. The characteristic feature of that system is the jSftnya-v&da, or nihilism, pure and simple. As such it is referred to and refuted in Gotama's Ny^ya-SHtras IV, 37 to 40, in6Kapila's S&mkhya-Sfttras I, 43, 44, in B&dar&ya-fta's Vedanta-Sutras II, 2, 28, where $amkara distinctly refers the doctrine that we know no objects, but only our perceptions of them, to Sugata or Buddha. The author of the Pa^&adasi quotes the M&dhyamikas by name as the teachers of universal nihilism (Sarvam $unyam).

If Nag&rgruna was really the author of the MadhyamikaSfttras, as we now possess them, they would carry us back to about the first century A.D., and we should have in his K&rikas, as explained by .BTandra-Kirtti, the oldest document of systematic philosophy in India, which will require very careful examination. Though it is different, no doubt, from all the six systems, it nevertheless shares in common with them many of the ideas and even technical terms. If it teaches the $unyatva or emptiness of the world, this after DIGNAGA. 367 all is not very different from the Ved&ntic Avidya, and the S&mkhya Aviveka, and if it teaches tHe Pratityatva of everything, that need be no more than the dependence of everything on something else1. The distinction made by the Madhyamikas between what is Paramarthika, real in the highest sense, and Samvr&tika, veiled, is much the same as the distinction of the later Vedanta between what is really real (ParamarthataA.), and what is Vyavaharika, phenomenal or the result of Maya, sometimes called Samvriti, the veil that covers the Nirgu^a Brahman or the Tad, which again is not very different from what the Buddhists meant originally by Sunya, empty, for they hold that even the Sunya is not altogether nothing. Many of the technical terms used by the Madhyamikas are the same as those with which we are acquainted in the other systems. DuAkha, pain, for instance, is divided^ into JLdhyatmika, intrinsic, Adhibhautika, extrinsic, and Adhidaivika, divine or supernatural. We meet with the five perceptions of colour, taste, smell, touch, and sound, and with their five causes, light, water, earth, air, and ether, and we also have the well-known idea that Manas, mind, forms the sixth sense. What is peculiar to the Buddhists is that to them neither the objects of sense nor the sensations point to an underlying substance or reality.

We owe a great debt of gratitude to both Sarat Chandra Das and $rl Satis Chandra Vidy&bhusha'tt/a for their labours in Tibet, and we look forward to many valuable contributions from their pen, more particularly for retranslations from Tibetan.

Whether Buddhist philosophy shares more in common with the S&mkhya than with the Nyaya and Vaiseshika seems to me as doubtful as ever. The fundamental position of the Samkhya, as Satk&ryavada, is the very opposite of the Buddhist view of the world.

1 Pratttya in Pratitya-samutpada and similar words may best be rendered by dependent or conditioned. A son, for instance, is a son, Pitaram Pratitya, dependent on a father, and a father is impossible without a son. In the same way everything is dependent on something else.

Bibliography, It was in 1852 that I published my first contributions to a study of Indian philosophy in the Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenldndischen Gesellschaft. These papers did not extend, however, beyond the Vaiseshika and Nyaya-philosophy as treated in the Tarkasamgraha, and more urgent occupations connected with the edition of the Rig-veda prevented me at the time from finishing what I had prepared for publication on the other systems of Indian philosophy. Though, of course, much new and important material has come to light in the meantime, particularly through the publications of the Vaiseshika- Sutras in the Bibliotheca Indica, through the complete translation of them by A. E. Gough, 1873, an<i through the comprehensive researches of European scholars, such as Professors Deussen and Garbe, I found that there was not much to alter in my old account of Gotama's and KaTiada's philosophies, as given in the German Oriental Journal, and in my paper on Indian Logic contributed to the late Archbishop Thomson's Laws of Thought. Indian philosophy has this great advantage that each tenet is laid down in the Sutras with the utmost precision, so that there can be little doubt as to what KaTiada or Gotama thought about the nature of the soul, the reality of human knowledge, the relation between cause and effect, the meaning of creation, and the relation between God or the Supreme Being and man. Thus it may be understood why even papers published so long ago as 1824, such as J. Colebrooke's papers on the Nyaya and Vaiseshika and the other systems of Indian philosophy, may still be recommended to all who want trustworthy information on Indian philosophy. These essays have sometimes been called antiquated, but there is a great difference between what is old and what is antiquated. The difficulty in giving an account of these systems for the benefit of European readers consists* far more in deciding what may be safely omitted, so as to bring out the salient points of each system, than in recapitulating all their tenets.
Books in which the Nyaya and Vaiseshika-systems may NYAYA-PHILOSOPHY. 369 be studied by those who are unacquainted with Sanskrit are, besides the papers of Colebrooke : —

Ballantyne, The Aphorisms of the Nyaya-Philosophy by Gautama, Sanskrit and English, Allahabad, 1 850. (Gautaina is the same as Gotama, only that by a tacit agreement Gotama has generally been used as the name of the philosopher, Gautama as that of Buddha, both belonging, it would seem, to the family of the Gautamas or Gotamas, the^MSS. varying with regard to the vowel.)
zh: 既非黑亦非白，它不產生任何果報，因為它是在沒有任何欲望的情況下履行的。

作為行動的結果，我們有薩摩沙那（Vasanas）、印象或三摩迦羅（Samskaras）、稟性。它們表現為要麼存留下來的東西（往往處於潛伏狀態），然後被稱為記憶¹，要麼表現為人、鳥、牛、婆羅門或首陀羅特有的類屬，以及一個人出生的地點和時間。這些殘留物永遠不會消失，因此動物的傾性可能在婆羅門身上潛伏一段時間，但當他進入犬類生命時就會再次爆發。據說它們並非無始的，因為欲望和恐懼只有在存在被恐懼或渴望的對象時才能出現（四、10）。印象由知覺引起，知覺源於欲望，欲望源於無明。它們全部的結果是具有本能的身體，它們的棲居所是心識，它們的依託——或者說它們所依靠的東西——與知覺的依託相同，即客觀世界。因此據說它們像種子一樣發芽，但也可以通過知識與瑜伽（Yoga）像焙過的種子一樣被消滅。在這個連貫的脈絡中，討論了一個問題：任何東西如何才能被完全摧毀，存在之物如何才能被化為不存在，以及不存在之物如何才能被化為存在。然而，我懷疑艾亨德拉·拉爾·米特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）是否正確（三、9，四、13），當他在帕坦伽利（Patañjali）的心識中發現了某種類似的理念論或邏各斯（logoi）時，並主張物體呈現於心識或影響心識的三種方式或道路（Adhvans）——即過去、現在和未來——相應於承認普遍的先於事物（universalia ante rem）的理念或原型、普遍的存在於事物之中（universalia in re）的本質，以及普遍的後於事物（universalia post rem）的我們心識中的概念。我坦言幾乎無法理解他的意思。應當永遠不要忘記，心識被帕坦伽利視為自身無意識的（不同於自我照明的自性（Svabhasa），四、18），並且只有通過它與純粹智性的普魯沙（Purusha）的結合，才在一段時間內變得有意識和有智慧。心意（Manas）僅僅接受來自知覺的意識，這種意識實際上來自於普魯沙，因此這裡 ¹ 這種記憶非常接近我們所稱的本能、稟性或未經教習的能力。

應當具有語源學上雖然略顯奇想的「意識」(con-scientia) 之定義，以及梵文 Sam-vid 的定義，即與心靈一同認知，亦即領悟心靈的印象 (Svapuddhi Samvedanam)。但雖然質多 (Citta) 是心 (Manas) 的產物，而非直接來自慧 (Buddhi)，然而這質多，當被觀者 (Purusha) 從一方觀見，並從另一方被所見之物所染著時，可以稱之為觀者之思想，雖然這乃是基於暫時的誤解而已。這質多復被許多往昔的印象 (Vasana) 所染著。它可被稱為原質 (Prakriti) 的最高形式，如此一來，它自身並無目的，但實則為他者而動作，即為觀者而動作，將觀者束縛並吸引一時，據說其唯一目的乃是將觀者重新引領至對其真實自我的最終認識 (IV, 24)。
解脫（Kaivalya）。

一旦達成這一點，普魯沙（Purusha）便知道他自己並非經驗者，既非知者亦非行動者；而當心識（Manas）或主動心開始感受到解脫境界（Kaivalya）的來臨時，它越來越向內轉向，遠離世界，以免妨礙普魯沙最高福樂的獲得。然而在疏於防守的時刻或禪定的間隙中，總是存在著退墮的危險。舊有的印象可能重新浮現，心識可能失去其穩定性，除非一再使用古老的瑜伽療法來消除所有的障礙。最後，完美的辨識力得到了回報，這種回報被稱為一個奇異的詞彙——法雲（Dharmamegha），即德行之雲。知識與德行形如因果般不可分離。一切作為與一切苦難如今都已止息，甚至所應知者亦漸漸變小，三德（GuṇAs），即原質（Prakriti），既已完成其功用，便不再困擾；普魯沙恢復其本性，獲得自在、寧靜、自由與福樂。

我需要指出，您提供的英文原文「Is Yog-a Mlfcilism ?」似乎包含排版錯誤或OCR掃描錯誤。「Mlfcilism」不是英文單詞。 根據穆勒1873年《宗教學導論》的內容脈絡，這句話可能應為： - "Is Yoga Mysticism?" （瑜伽是神祕主義嗎？） 或 - "Is Yoga Nihilism?" （瑜伽是虛無主義嗎？） 懇請您提供正確的英文原文，以便我提供準確的繁體中文翻譯。
這是瑜伽哲學的終極，難怪它應該被誤認為是徹底的虛無主義。

庫森及其他人士的著作。但首先，雖然對於已獲得真知的我們的神我（Purusha）而言，物質根源（Prakriti）的運動已經停止，但據信它將永遠為無數其他神我的利益而繼續進行；只要還有任何觀者存在，物質根源的景象就永遠不會終止。其次，神我雖然已從幻相中解脫，但並不因此而湮滅。他自身獨立於自然而存在，而且雖然並未明確表述，但神我在其孤絕狀態中可能會繼續生存，如同吠檀多傳統中的離身解脫者（jivanmukta），在眾多奴隸的擁擠中保持自身的自由，不存有對另一生命的恐懼或希望，在此永恆流變的輪迴（Samsara）中保持自身不變。然而，我們毋須試圖填補帕坦伽利本人所沉默以對的地方。無論是瑜伽、數論，乃至於吠檀多和佛教的最終目標，始終無法被描述。涅槃（Nirvana）在其最高意義上是一個名字和一個概念，但對它不能作出任何謂述。它是「眼睛所未曾見，心思所未曾進入」的。我們知道它存在；但沒有人能夠說出它究竟是什麼，那些試圖如此做的人往往傾向於將其化約為一種虛幻的變相或虛無。

儘管我希望前面的概述能夠正確傳達瑜伽哲學（Yoga-philosophy）的總體傾向，但我也深切明白，有許多要點需要進一步闡明，即使是本土的闡述者對此也持有不同的見解。在所有這些研究中，我們必須警惕的是：不可因為無法立即理解某事物，或它在我們看來似乎異想天開或不合理，就將其摒棄為荒誕之談。根據我自身的經驗，我深知有多少次那些長期以來在我看來毫無意義、甚至荒誕的事物，經過時間的推移，竟然揭示出遠遠超越我預期的深層意蘊。

這眾多的技術術語雖然可能令我們感到困惑，但不能完全予以抑制，因為它有助於顯示這些印度思想體系必然經歷了多麼漫長而持續的發展，才有後來由巴坦伽利（Patañjali）及其他人所做的、將其化為系統秩序的嘗試。我心中保有一項堅強的信念，即印度哲學家在其推理中是誠實的，決不使用空洞的言辭。但仍有許多工作待做，我只能寄望於若有他人步我之後塵，他們終將使這些古老的骸骨重獲生機。這些古代賢哲應當成為我們在思想的未知大陸上的共同工作者與共同探險者，我們不應該害怕跟隨他們的蹤跡。他們總是對自己的信念懷有勇氣，若其不可避免地遵循自己的前提，他們就不會退縮於任何後果。這就是為什麼我懷疑巴坦伽利所承認的伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）或主宰—與否認存在任何支持此類存有之論證的迦毗羅（Kapila）相對—是否應該被視為純粹的權宜之計或對通俗輿論的遷就。印度哲學家是誠實的，巴坦伽利在《瑜伽經》第二篇第36經中明言真理勝於祭祀。他們可能會犯錯，就如柏拉圖曾經犯錯，甚至康德也曾犯錯，但他們並非欺人而自欺者，他們既不欺騙自己或說服自己，也不試圖欺騙他人。
我注意到您提供的文本「SatyapratishifMyam kriyaphalasrayatvat」似乎不是英文原文，而是梵文（或梵文的拉丁轉寫形式）。 根據您的翻譯規則，我應當「只翻譯英文原文；附上的德文僅供消歧義參考，**不要翻譯德文**」。這條規則在邏輯上也應適用於其他非英文的外語文本。 **請您提供英文原文**，我將按照您的所有規則進行準確的繁體中文翻譯。
雖然在迄今所考察的各派系中，特別是在吠檀多、數論和瑜伽派，貫穿著強烈的宗教性乃至詩意的脈動，但我們現在轉向兩個系統——尼亞亞與瓦伊舍希卡——它們則非常乾澀而缺乏想像力，更像是我們所謂的經院哲學系統，即對可知事物的務實論述：一方面是圍繞我們的世界，或內在的世界，亦即我們知覺、概念化或推理的各種能力或力量；另一方面是這些能力所呈現給我們的對象。

應該記住，就像數論派（Samkhya）和瑜伽派（Yoga）一樣，以及在某種程度上像前吠檀多派（Purva Mimamsa）和後吠檀多派（Uttara Mimamsa）一樣，紐亞派（Nyaya）和瓦伊舍什卡派（Vaiseshika）也被印度人本身視為構成同一門學科。我們確實擁有獨立的《紐亞經》（Nyaya-Sutras）和另一部《瓦伊舍什卡經》（Vaiseshika-Sutras），這些經典及其公認的作者高多摩（Gotama）和卡那德（Kanada），長期以來一直被視為這兩支古代印度哲學流派所源出的原始根源。但我們現在知道，在我所謂的經文時期——即可能要歸於最初將書寫而非純粹記誦文學付諸實踐的時代——自然而然產生的文學風格，絕非局限於那個古老的時期，而是在後世得到了如此良好的模仿，以至於我們發現它不僅在比較晚於馬德瓦（約西元1350年）的《數論經》中被大為成功地運用，在更現代的著作中也是如此。應該始終銘記在心的是，歸於高多摩和卡那德的《經》預設了哲學思想之前漫長的發展過程，而不是將這兩者視為兩股獨立的潮流，似乎更有可能的是，在最初存在著一個尚未分化的、半哲學半通俗的思想體系，涉及可以被認知的事物——即類（Padarthas，亦即「一切可知之物」——以及獲取此等知識的手段，從這個體系出發，在後來的時期中，根據其中一個或另一個主題的優勢地位，才分化出了瓦伊舍什卡派和紐亞派這兩個系統。這兩個系統當然共享許多共同點，因此我們完全可以理解為什麼在後來它們應該被重新拉近並被視為一體，正如我們在希瓦迪蒂亞的《七類論》（約西元1400年）、《語言評判》（Bhasha-Pariccheda）及其註釋《解脫論》（Muktavali）、《邏輯要略》（Tarkasamgraha）、《邏輯手冊》（Tarkakamudi）、《邏輯精華》（Tarkamrita）等著作中所見的那樣。出於實踐的目的，跟隨它們的例子無疑是更可取的，從而避免了必須討論同樣主題兩次的必要性。可能曾經有過一部古老的《邏輯論》（Tarka），非常類似於我們的《邏輯要略》——一部是在古老的《觀察論》（Anvikshiki）系統分化之前，另一部是在這兩者匯合之後。但這些迄今為止都僅是推測而已，可能始終只能保持為純粹的推測，因此，在我們知識的現狀下，並且如我們所必須做的那樣，主要依靠現存的《經》作為印度本身所承認的權威，我們不應該嘗試進行歷史性的論述，而應該儘管會有不可避免的重複，仍然將每一個系統分開來討論。

一位非常熱誠的本地學者摩訶提婆‧羅者‧博達斯（Mahadeo Rājāram Bodas），在他所編《論集》（Tarkasamgraha）的導論中，確實承諾要為我們提供某種印度尼夜耶哲學的歷史。但不幸的是，在尼夜耶的歷史發展中對我們最具興趣的那個時期，即先於《尼夜耶經》（Nyaya-Sutras）成書的時期，也被他留作空白，理由很簡單，就是「關於喬答摩之前的尼夜耶，一無所知」。然而，後來的時期已經被博達斯先生極其妥當地處理過了，我可以引導讀者們參閱他的著作，以獲得該主題最完善的資訊。博達斯先生將喬答摩的《經》置於西元前五世紀或四世紀；而他表示
一種信念，認為勝論派（Vaiseshika）、乃至數論派（Sāṃkhya）作為思想體系，都先於佛陀存在，然而卻未能提出任何新穎或確定的證據。

日期是印度文學史上的薄弱環節，在我們目前研究的狀況下，任何日期，無論多晚，都應該令人欣然接受。在過去的年月裡，要將《迦毗羅經》（Kapila-Sutras）劃定在西元第十四世紀甚至第十五世紀，似乎就像是直率的異端邪說。迦梨陀娑（Kālidāsa）本人不就被指派到遠先於我們紀元開始的時期嗎？現在似乎普遍認可，迦梨陀娑實際上屬於西元第六世紀，這個迦梨陀娑的日期可能會幫助我們確定高多摩（Gotama）經論的日期，這對我們來說是有價值的，儘管那些想像梵文文學價值主要取決於其假定的遠古性的人可能會輕視它。我曾經指出¹，根據本土解釋者的說法，迦梨陀娑在其《雲使》（Meghaduta）的一首詩句中提及了邏輯學家無著（Dignāga）。因此我們可以推測，無著被認為是迦梨陀娑的同時代人。現在，根據瓦恰斯帕蒂·米什拉（Vāchaspati Misra）在其《尼耶亞評註義疏》（Nyaya-varttika-tatparyatīkā）中的記載，無著曾以異端或佛教的意義解釋高多摩的《尼耶亞箴言》（Nyaya aphorisms），而烏德約塔卡拉（Uddyotakara）撰寫其評註是為了駁斥他的解釋，並恢復帕克希拉斯瓦敏（Pakshilasvamin）的解釋。如果瓦恰斯帕蒂·米什拉是對的，我們就應該被允許將無著置於第六世紀，並對高多摩的《經論》劃定同樣的時期，或者更早的時期，因為那是由他和其他尼耶亞哲學家所解釋的。如此晚的日期或許看起來不值一提，但我認為它仍然是值得擁有的。如我在上面引用的段落中試圖展示的那樣，還有其他幾個日期可以借由無著的日期而確定（《印度》，第307頁以下）。
對佛教文獻的更全面研究，如果我的推測正確的話，可能會進一步照亮婆羅門後期文獻的年代學問題。我認為佛陀的追隨者在最初並未在印度造成決裂。¹ ¹ 印度，第307頁。
參見薩蒂斯‧昌德拉‧維迪亞布尚那（Satis Chandra Vidyabushana）教授著，《佛教文獻學會雜誌》第四卷，第三及四部分，第16頁。

迪格那格（Dignaga）注疏梵文原典的方式與婆羅門的文獻傳統之間，並不如通常所假設的那樣完全相符。在印度哲學的各個體系中，佛教徒之所以對兩派彌曼薩學派關注甚少，是很容易理解的，因為這兩派都涉及《吠陀》，而佛教徒已經拒絕了這一權威。但佛教徒沒有理由放棄對紐亞亞學派（Nyāya）或瓦伊舍希卡學派的研究，甚至也沒有理由放棄對數論學派的研究，雖然他們在某些點上保留異議，例如伊濕瓦羅（Īśvara）的存在問題，紐亞亞學派承認它，但釋迦牟尼佛否定它。我們知道在哈爾沙王朝的宮廷裡，婆羅門教徒、佛教徒和耆那教徒同樣受到歡迎（見《印度》第307頁及以後）。我們從中國旅行者如玄奘那裡得知，例如無著（Vasubandha）在成為佛教徒之前，曾跟隨他的師父毘奈耶跋陀羅（Vinayabhadra）或僧伽跋陀羅（Saṃghabhadra）學習，不僅學習了十八個佛教學派的著作，還學習了六部蒂爾塔亞（Tīrthya）哲學體系，這明確指的是六個婆羅門教哲學體系。這位無著在很高的年紀時，實際上是玄奘的老師，玄奘於公元629年至648年在印度旅行。因此在無著的時代，印度的所有六個哲學體系必定已經以經文（Sūtra）或頌文（Kārikā）的形式存在。我們至少在一個案例中掌握了帕克希拉·斯瓦敏（Pakṣila-svāmin）或瓦茨亞亞那（Vātsyāyana）對《紐亞亞經》的註疏，與我們現今擁有的經文相同，而我們知道同樣的這些經文後來也被佛教徒迪格那格解釋過。這部佛教註疏遭到六世紀婆羅門教徒烏迪約塔卡羅（Uddyotakara）的批評，而在七世紀初，據說佛教徒法稱（Dharmakīrti）為迪格那格進行了辯護，並批評了烏迪約塔卡羅的《紐亞亞釋論》（Nyāyavārttika）。在九世紀，佛教徒法護（Dharmottara）為法稱進行辯護，並間接地為迪格那格對《紐亞亞經》的詮釋進行辯護，直到十世紀
2 雖然迪格那迦（Dignaga）的著作迄今尚未被發現，但斯里‧薩拉特‧錢德拉指出，大喇嘛的圖書館中存有他的《正理集》（Nyaya-samuccaya）的藏文譯本（《佛教文獻學會期刊》，第三部分，1896年，第17頁）。

世紀時，瓦茨帕蒂‧米什拉（Vaśpati Misra）終於在其《尼耶亞釋義疏》（Nyāya-vārttikatātparyatīkā）中重新確立了婆羅門對尼耶亞學派的見解。這將與婆羅門的反動時期及佛教在印度的全面衰落相符，因此為我們呈現了從六世紀至十世紀期間，婆羅門與佛教徒在尼耶亞學派研究中可以理解的進展，而尼耶亞學派的復興則始於十四世紀時居住在密提拉（Mithilā）的甘傑沙‧烏帕迪亞亞（Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya）。

感謝沙拉特‧錢德拉‧達斯（Sarat Chandra Dás）和薩蒂斯‧錢德拉‧維迪亞布胡沙納（Satis Chandra Vidyābhushana）的辛勤工作，我們近來得以接觸到佛教哲學各派的若干經論（Sutras），這些著作充滿了學術價值。在佛教四大哲學派——中觀派（M&dhyamika）、瑜伽行派（YogaMra）、經量部（Sautr&ntika）和毘婆沙派（VaibMshika）中，第一派即中觀派已由月稱（Jfandra-Kirtti）所著的《中觀論釋》（Madhyamika Vritti）呈現在我們眼前，且吾人有充分理由期待其他哲學著作，例如《因明論》（Ny&ya-samui&aya），也能藉由這些孜孜不倦的學者之努力而為我們所獲得。

中觀學派的經文（Sutra）或更準確地說是頌文（Kārikā）當然必須與其所欲闡釋的思想體系有所區別。該體系的特徵在於空性說（śūnya-vāda），或者說純粹的虛無主義。作為這樣的學說，它在高塔摩的《因明經》（Nyāya-Sūtras）第四卷第37至40條、在迦毘羅的《數論經》（Sāṃkhya-Sūtras）第一卷第43、44條、以及在婆達羅耶那的《韋檀多經》（Vedānta-Sūtras）第二卷第2、28條中被提及並駁斥。在上述各處，商羯羅明確地將我們不知物自身，僅知其知覺之說這一教義歸諸蘇迦陀或佛陀。《百相論》（Pañcapadī）的作者按名字引用中觀論者，將其視為普遍虛無主義（Sarvam Śūnyam）的倡導者。

如果那伽阿周那（Nāgārjuna）確實是我們現存《中論》（Madhyamika-Sfttras）的作者，那麼這些著作將把我們帶回約西元一世紀，而我們將在他的《迴諍論》（K&rikas）中看到——由月稱（Candrakirtti）所闡釋——印度最古老的系統哲學文獻，這將需要非常仔細的檢視。儘管它無疑不同於所有六大系統，然而它與它們在許多思想甚至技術術語上仍有共通之處。如果它教導了空性（Sunyatva）或世界的虛無性，這畢竟與吠檀多學派的無明（Avidya）以及數論學派的無智（Aviveka）並無太大差異；如果它教導了一切事物的緣起性（Pratityatva），這不過是一切事物對他物的依賴而已。中觀論者在勝義諦（Paramarthika）——最高意義上的真實——與世俗諦（Samvr&tika）——被遮蔽的——之間所作的區分，與後期吠檀多學派在真正的真實（Paramarthata）與世俗諦（Vyavaharika）——現象性的或摩耶（Maya）的結果——之間所作的區分大致相同，有時也被稱為世俗諦（Samvriti），遮蔽無性梵（Nirguna Brahman）或彼（Tad）的帷幕，而這同樣並無太大差異於佛教徒最初以空（Sunya）、即空無所指的含義——因為他們認為即使空性本身也並非絕對虛無。中觀論者所使用的許多技術術語與我們在其他系統中所熟悉的術語相同。例如苦（Duhkha）被劃分為內在的（Adhyatmika）、外在的（Adhibhautika）與神聖的或超越的（Adhidaivika）。我們遇到了色、味、香、觸、聲五種知覺，以及它們的五種成因——光、水、土、風與空——我們也看到了眾所周知的觀念，即心（Manas）構成第六感。佛教徒所特有的是，對於他們而言，感官的對象與感受皆不指向任何基礎實體或真實。
我們對薩拉特‧錢德拉‧達斯（Sarat Chandra Das）與厄爾‧薩蒂斯‧錢德拉‧維迪亞布舒山塔（Satis Chandra Vidyabhushana）在西藏所做的工作深感感謝，並期待他們日後提供更多寶貴的著述，尤其是來自藏文的重新翻譯。
佛教哲學是否與數論派（Sāṃkhya）的共同之處更多於與尼耶亞派（Nyaya）和吠世師派（Vaiśeṣika），在我看來仍然令人懷疑。數論派的根本立場，即現果論（Satk&ryavāda），恰好與佛教的世界觀相反。
《緣起》（Pratitya-samutpada）中的「緣」（Pratitya）及類似詞語最宜譯為「依存的」或「條件性的」。例如，一個兒子之所以為兒子，乃是依存於父親（Pitaram Pratitya），而父親的存在亦不可能不依於兒子。同理，萬物皆依存於他物。

1852 年，我在《德國東方學會雜誌》(*Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenländischen Gesellschaft*) 上發表了我對印度哲學研究的首批論文。然而，這些論文的範圍並未超越《因明集論》(*Tarkasamgraha*) 中所論述的勝論派與正理派哲學，當時由於編纂《梨俱吠陀》(*Rig-Veda*) 版本的更為迫切的工作，我無法完成原先為出版而準備的關於其他印度哲學體系的研究。誠然，自那以後已有許多新的重要文獻問世，特別是通過《博學叢書》(*Bibliotheca Indica*) 中刊行的《勝論經》(*Vaiseshika-Sutras*)，以及 A. E. 高夫 (A. E. Gough) 於 1873 年對其進行的完整譯本，還有歐洲學者（如德森 (Deussen) 教授與葛比 (Garbe) 教授）進行的全面研究，我發現在我刊登於《德國東方雜誌》中關於高多摩 (Gotama) 與卡那達 (Kaṇāda) 哲學的舊論述中，以及在我為已故湯姆森大主教 (Archbishop Thomson) 所著《思維定律》(*Laws of Thought*) 一書所撰關於印度邏輯的論文中，實無太多需要改動之處。印度哲學具有這樣的重大優勢：每一項學說都在《經》(*Sutras*) 中以最高的精確性闡述，因此對於卡那達或高多摩關於靈魂的本質、人類知識的真實性、因果關係、創造的意義以及上帝或最高存在與人之間的關係等問題的看法，幾乎不可能有什麼疑問。由此可見，為何即使是早至 1824 年所發表的論文——例如科爾布魯克 (J. Colebrooke) 關於正理派、勝論派及其他印度哲學體系的論文——仍然可被推薦給所有希望獲得關於印度哲學之可信信息的讀者。這些論文有時被稱為已過時，但舊的東西與過時的東西之間存在著巨大的差別。為歐洲讀者闡述這些體系的困難，遠非在於羅列它們的所有學說，而在於決定什麼可以安全地省略，以便突出每一體系的顯著特點。
對於不懂梵文者而言，可用以研習紐亞雅（Nyaya）與瓦依舍希卡（Vaiseshika）體系的書籍，除了寇爾布魯克（Colebrooke）的論文外，尚有：——

Ballantyne, The Aphorisms of the Nyaya-Philosophy by Gautama, Sanskrit and English, Allahabad, 1850. (Gautama 與 Gotama 相同，只是按照約定俗成的慣例，Gotama 通常被用作該位哲學家的名字，而 Gautama 則用作佛陀的名字，兩者似乎都屬於 Gautama 或 Gotama 家族，而手稿中關於母音的記載有所不同。)
